interactions.summary: Jiang completes the Roman duty model by saying Creusa's death frees Aeneas for empire and shields him from embarrassment; this is the Roman perception of wifely duty.
The student reads Creusa's ghost explaining that the gods forbid her departure, that Aeneas must accept exile, kingdom, and a new queen, and that she will not become a Greek slave.
Jiang closes by contrasting Odyssey love with Aeneid piety: if love is hell, heaven is obedience to divine prophecy, even when Jupiter demands sacrifice and separation.

speaker_notes.note: No substantive student question appears at the end; Jiang asks "any questions" as classroom closure, with no answer in the transcript.

claims.claim: Creusa's death is framed by Jiang as useful to Aeneas because it frees him to marry into a new lineage and avoids the embarrassment of a wife enslaved by Greeks.
Jiang summarizes the Roman perception of wifely duty as self-erasure when a wife is no longer useful to the husband's destiny.
claims.temporal_scope: Dated normative diagnosis of the Aeneid's value system.

claims.claim: In Jiang's contrast, the Odyssey is the story of Penelope and Odysseus finding each other because love is the greatest force in the universe, whereas the Aeneid makes love hell.
Heaven in the Aeneid is piety: obedience to divine prophecy, including the prophecy that Troy must be destroyed so Rome can be created and Aeneas can found it.
The Aeneid reverses the Odyssey because the gods no longer will the lovers' reunion; they will Aeneas and Creusa's separation for the sake of Rome.
Civilization is a set of values and ideas that guide life, and the Aeneid becomes influential because it installs an inverted, poisoned Homeric value system at Roman scale.

signature_moments.moment: Wifely duty is compressed into a brutal imperative: if you are not useful to the husband's destiny, disappear.
signature_moments.source_phrase: If you're not useful to your husband, just kill yourself
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It preserves the force of Jiang's indictment of Roman domestic order.

signature_moments.moment: If love is hell, heaven is piety: obedience to prophecy rather than reunion with the beloved.
signature_moments.source_phrase: If love is hell, what is heaven? Heaven is piety.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the closing theological inversion of the episode.

signature_moments.moment: The gods who once brought Odysseus and Penelope together now demand that Aeneas and Creusa separate.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the gods willed that Chryssa and Aeneas separate
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes divine will itself carry the anti-Odyssey reversal.

signature_moments.moment: Civilization is not infrastructure first; it is a set of values and ideas that guide life.
signature_moments.source_phrase: what is civilization but a set of values a set of ideas
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The final definition explains why a poem can alter a civilization.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: In Jiang's reading of Roman values, a wife should remove herself if she burdens the husband's destiny or honor.
The Aeneid's positive value: obedience to divine prophecy and imperial destiny rather than love.
A set of values and ideas that guide life; therefore poetry can found or corrupt civilization.
The divine plan that Troy be destroyed and Aeneas suffer exile so Rome can be founded.

chronology_notes.note: This source closes the 2026-03-18 Great Books #7 lecture by making the anti-Homer thesis explicitly civilizational: the Aeneid guides life by installing inverted Homeric values.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR alternates Creusa/Ryusa/Chryssa and Ilias; semantic pass treats these as Creusa and Ascanius/Iulus contextually without correcting transcript text.
